questions.text: How many distinct values can be stored in a single 8-bit byte?
questions.explanation: An $n$-bit pattern can represent $2^n$ distinct values; for $n=8$ that is $2^8 = 256$ (denary $0$ to $255$).

questions.text: Convert the 8-bit binary number $01011010$ to denary.
questions.explanation: Only the bits set to 1 contribute their place value: $64 + 16 + 8 + 2 = 90$.

questions.text: Convert the denary number 90 to hexadecimal (two hex digits, e.g. write it like "c8").
questions.explanation: $90 \div 16 = 5$ remainder $10$. The remainder $10$ is hex digit A, and the quotient 5 is hex digit 5, giving $\texttt{5A}$. Check: $5 \times 16 + 10 = 90$.

questions.text: Convert the hexadecimal number $\texttt{2F}$ to denary.
questions.explanation: Each hex digit is worth its value times $16^{\text{position}}$: $2 \times 16 + 15 \times 1 = 32 + 15 = 47$ (recall $\texttt{F} = 15$).

questions.text: What is the main reason programmers write memory addresses and colour codes in hexadecimal rather than binary?
choices: Each hex digit represents exactly 4 bits, so a long binary string becomes a short, easy-to-read hex string via a simple mechanical grouping
Computer hardware performs arithmetic faster in hexadecimal than in binary
Hexadecimal is the only base a CPU's circuits can physically store
Hexadecimal removes the need for two's complement when representing negative numbers
questions.explanation: Because $16 = 2^4$, every hex digit corresponds to exactly one group of 4 bits (a nibble), so binary-to-hex conversion is a mechanical grouping, not real arithmetic — this is purely for human readability; the hardware still stores and processes binary.

questions.text: Add the 8-bit binary numbers $01011010$ and $00100101$. Give the 8-bit binary result (no spaces).
questions.explanation: $01011010$ (90) plus $00100101$ (37) equals $127$ in denary, and $127 = 01111111$ in 8-bit binary. No carry is generated out of bit 7, so there is no overflow.

questions.text: An 8-bit unsigned register adds $200$ and $100$ (binary $11001000 + 01100100$). What happens?
choices: The correct result, 300, is stored exactly
The result overflows: only the lower 8 bits (denary 44) are kept in the register, with a carry out that is discarded
The register automatically grows to 9 bits to fit the answer
The addition is invalid and the processor raises an error
questions.explanation: $200 + 100 = 300$, which needs 9 bits ($100101100$). An 8-bit register keeps only the lower 8 bits, $00101100 = 44$, and the leading bit is lost as a discarded carry — this is overflow, since $300 - 256 = 44$.

questions.text: Using 8-bit two's complement, what denary value does the bit pattern $11011011$ represent?
questions.explanation: The most significant bit contributes $-128$; the remaining bits $1011011$ contribute $64+16+8+2+1=91$. Total: $-128 + 91 = -37$.

questions.text: Find the 8-bit two's complement representation of $-37$. Give your answer as an 8-bit binary string (no spaces).
questions.explanation: $37$ in 8-bit binary is $00100101$. Invert every bit to get $11011010$, then add 1 to get $11011011$.

questions.text: Using 8-bit two's complement addition (discarding any carry out of bit 7), what denary value results from computing $50 + (-37)$?
questions.explanation: $50 = 00110010$ and $-37 = 11011011$. Adding these 8-bit patterns and discarding the carry out of bit 7 gives $00001101 = 8+4+1 = 13$, matching $50 - 37 = 13$.

questions.text: What is the range of denary values representable in 8-bit two's complement?
questions.explanation: An $n$-bit two's complement register represents $-2^{n-1}$ to $2^{n-1}-1$; for $n=8$ that is $-128$ to $127$ — the same 256 bit patterns as unsigned, shared differently between negative and positive values.

questions.text: A simplified floating-point format uses a sign bit, a 4-bit normalised mantissa (representing $0.bbbb$), and a 3-bit two's complement exponent. Given sign $=0$, mantissa $=1011$, exponent $=011$, what denary value does this represent?
questions.explanation: Mantissa $0.1011 = 0.5+0.125+0.0625 = 0.6875$. Exponent $011$ in 3-bit two's complement is $3$. Value $= 0.6875 \times 2^3 = 5.5$.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about character encoding are true?
choices: ASCII uses 7 bits and can represent 128 characters
Extended ASCII uses 8 bits and can represent 256 characters
Unicode/UTF-8 can represent far more characters than ASCII because it can use more bytes for less common characters
Every character set must use exactly the same number of bits for every character
questions.explanation: ASCII is a 7-bit code ($2^7=128$ characters); extended ASCII is 8-bit ($2^8=256$ characters); UTF-8 is variable-length (1 to 4 bytes), which is exactly how it covers far more characters than a fixed 1-byte scheme could. The last statement is false — UTF-8 is a counterexample, since it deliberately varies the number of bytes per character.

questions.text: An uncompressed image is 200 pixels wide by 100 pixels tall, stored with 8 bits of colour depth per pixel. What is the file size in bytes?
questions.explanation: Size in bits $= 200 \times 100 \times 8 = 160{,}000$ bits. Dividing by 8 gives bytes: $160{,}000 \div 8 = 20{,}000$ bytes.

questions.text: A 10-second mono sound clip is sampled at 44,100 Hz with a bit depth of 16 bits. What is the uncompressed file size in bytes?
questions.explanation: Size in bits $= 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 10 = 7{,}056{,}000$ bits. Dividing by 8 gives bytes: $7{,}056{,}000 \div 8 = 882{,}000$ bytes.

questions.text: Why does floating-point represent both very large and very small numbers more flexibly than fixed-point, at the cost of some precision?
choices: Because the exponent scales the value over a huge range, while a fixed-length mantissa always supplies roughly the same number of significant bits regardless of magnitude
Because floating-point numbers are always stored using more total bits than fixed-point numbers
Because floating-point cannot represent negative numbers, simplifying the hardware
Because fixed-point cannot represent fractional values at all
questions.explanation: Floating-point's exponent lets the same bit pattern length cover an enormous range of magnitudes by shifting the effective position of the binary point, but the mantissa still only ever supplies a fixed number of significant bits — so very large and very small values are represented with the same relative precision, unlike fixed-point where the position of the point (and hence range vs precision) is fixed in advance.
